narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings—pouring your heart into law school dreams only to face this setback feels deeply personal, and I'm here to walk you through why our predictive model saw your path to the bar exam as a challenging one, with about a 70% chance of not passing. Picture your journey as a story the model pieced together from key chapters of your background: it started with your Year 1 grades landing you in the 2nd decile at school, a early sign of struggling to stand out among peers that weighed heavily as the second-biggest factor pulling toward difficulty ahead. Then came Year 3, where another 2nd decile finish became the strongest influence of all, painting a picture of consistent hurdles in keeping up as the workload intensified. Your LSAT score of 40 followed close behind as a notable concern, suggesting the analytical edge needed for bar success might be tough to sharpen. Layered on top was your upper-middle family income, which the model linked to added pressures that haven't always translated to smoother academic sailing for others in similar spots. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.1 added to this narrative thread, reinforcing a pattern rather than breaking it. Even elements like being a white female pursuing full-time studies tipped the scales slightly toward the model's worries, based on patterns it's seen before. Together, these pieces formed a compelling story of potential roadblocks, not a judgment on your worth or effort, but a cautious forecast to guide tough admission choices. The good news? This isn't the end of your story—you've got time to rewrite those deciles with targeted prep, boost that LSAT, or build standout experiences that shift the odds. Many have turned similar predictions around through grit and strategy. Hang in there; your determination can change the ending.
